pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - CNN's Call to Earth initiative

pageCaption.value: Call to Earth is CNN’s commitment to reporting on the environmental challenges facing our planet, together with the solutions.

WaPageTitle.value: Call to Earth - CNN

metadescription.value: CNN has partnered with Rolex in a shared commitment for a sustainable future. Learn more about the initiative on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Call to Earth - CNN

text.value:
Call to Earth is CNN’s commitment to reporting on the environmental challenges facing our planet, together with the solutions.

In partnership with Rolex as part of their Perpetual Planet initiative, our goal is to drive awareness, action and education around key issues, inspiring a blueprint for a more environmentally conscious life.

title.value: Teaching kids to teach about the planet

description.value: The Yucatán Peninsula where Maritza Morales Casanova lives is under pressure from two million inhabitants and surging numbers of visitors. Since the age of 10, she has cared about the environment, launching the award-winning advocacy group HUNAB. Now, driven by a sense of urgency over the need for the next generation to safeguard their natural resources, she is building an environmental education theme park, Ceiba Pentandra.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - Children hold the key to climate change

pageCaption.value:
As young children, most of us don't know what we want to be when we grow up.
Not Maritza Morales Casanova.

WaPageTitle.value: ‘Kids are the best teachers’: Why Maritza Morales Casanova believes children are the answer to climate change

metadescription.value: Once a child activist, Maritza Morales Casanova is now teaching younger generations about nature to inspire environmental change. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Why Maritza Morales Casanova believes children are the answer to climate change

title.value: Protecting the biodiversity of Sierra Gorda

description.value: Mexico’s Sierra Gorda is one of Mexico’s most ecologically diverse protected areas. Here, Martha Isabel Ruiz Corzo created much needed economic opportunities through protection of the environment. For many families living in poverty, her system helped find them paid jobs in reforestation, waste management, eco-tourism and education.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - Photography as a tool for conservation

pageCaption.value: Growing up in the remote mountains of Sierra Gorda, Mexico, Roberto Pedraza Ruiz developed a serious case of biophilia.

WaPageTitle.value: Growing up wild: This photographer captures the beauty of Mexico's mountains

metadescription.value: Award-winning photographer Roberto Pedraza Ruiz works alongside his family to protect the ancient Mexican forests he grew up in. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Growing up wild: This photographer captures the beauty of Mexico's mountains

description.value: Rolex Awards Laureate Sarah Toumi has achieved grass roots success in combating desertification with her scheme to plant Acacia trees in Tunisia. Now she joins the Great Green Wall accelerator with a plan to transform degraded landscapes across the entire continent, by growing a 5,000-mile living wall.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - The Great Green Wall

pageCaption.value: The world's largest desert is growing. In the last century, the Sahara Desert expanded by more than 10%, now covering an area of more than 3.3 million square miles (8.6 million square kilometers) and spanning 11 countries in the north of Africa.

WaPageTitle.value: Holding back the world’s largest desert

metadescription.value: Rolex Awards Laureate Sarah Toumi joins the Great Green Wall accelerator and plans to transform landscapes of the entire continent. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Holding back the world’s largest desert

title.value: NASA technology helps save the world's largest shark

description.value: Thousands of "citizen scientists" around the world are helping to save endangered whale sharks by taking photos of them for an online photo identification library. Co-founded by marine biologist Brad Norman, The Wildbook for Whale Sharks uses NASA technology to identify individual sharks by the patterns on their skin, providing critical information about their population hotspots and migration routes.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - Saving the world's largest fish

pageCaption.value: Thousands of people around the world are lending a hand to help save the world’s biggest fish.

WaPageTitle.value: How NASA technology can help save whale sharks

metadescription.value: Rolex Laureate Brad Norman has created a photo-identification system inspired by NASA technology to help protect whale sharks. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: How NASA technology can help save whale sharks

description.value: Marine biologist and Rolex Associate Laureate Emma Camp is a leading researcher of coral conservation and resilience at the University of Technology Sydney — fighting to save the marine wonders she was first captivated by as a child. Her work involves searching the globe to find and study the world's toughest and most resilient corals — the species with the best chance of surviving the climate crisis. “Time is running out and it is urgent," she says. "If we don't act, these critically important ecosystems... are going to be lost or at least severely degraded to a point where we actually can't go back.”

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - On a mission to save dying reefs

pageCaption.value: This marine biologist wants to use the world's toughest corals to save dying reefs

WaPageTitle.value: Toughest corals to save dying reefs

metadescription.value: Marine biologist Emma Camp is researching coral conservation and resilience to save reefs degraded by climate change. Learn more on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Toughest corals to save dying reefs

description.value: Environmentalist and Rolex Awards Laureate Arun Krishnamurthy runs the Environmentalist Foundation of India (EFI), a nonprofit group that restores freshwater lakes and ponds across India. Over the last 13 years, EFI has carried out restoration work on 112 lakes and ponds across India. Outreach is important, he says, and that conservation should be exciting and motivational. “I cannot just walk into any neighborhood and start cleaning up a lake or a pond until the community there has a buy-in ... if they are willing to participate,” he says. “Otherwise, it's just my responsibility to clean it up, and tomorrow it will go back to where it was before.”

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - Cleaning up India's Lakes

pageCaption.value: From Google to garbage disposal: the environmentalist cleaning up India's lakes

WaPageTitle.value: Cleaning up India's Lakes

metadescription.value: Rolex Laureate Arun Krishnamurthy and his non-profit organisation are helping to tackle India's water problems. Discover how on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Cleaning up India's Lakes

description.value: Chilean explorer and photographer, Cristian Donoso, has spent over two decades traversing the wilds of Patagonia, Antarctica and the Arctic to capture images showing the impacts of climate change. The Rolex Associate Laureate says that he shares the same passion as the 19th and 20th century naturalists who have inspired his work. But the world he studies looks very different. His aim is to engage and educate people about the impact of global warming in faraway places. “Through art, through the vision of photography, we offer a clearer perspective, with a very direct and sensitive message,” he says.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - A century of climate change in Patagonia

pageCaption.value: Explorer's mission to photograph a century of climate change in Patagonia

WaPageTitle.value: A century of climate change in Patagonia

metadescription.value: Chilean Rolex Laureate Cristian Donoso has spent over two decades capturing the impacts of climate change in Patagonia. Learn more on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: A century of climate change in Patagonia

description.value: Chilean-German biologist and Rolex Awards Laureate Vreni Häussermann arrived in Chilean Patagonia in the late 1990s to explore what she calls “one of the last wildernesses on Earth.” Together with fellow scientist and husband Gunter Fosterra, they have discovered more than 100 new species of sea life, including many corals and anemones. But they say the ecosystem has changed profoundly since they began. “It's really hard to track what is the reason for each change, but we're definitely seeing drastic changes in biodiversity.” She believes the changes “are definitely too fast to be natural.”

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - Discovering new species in Chilean Patagonia

pageCaption.value: The wild deep: Discovering new species in Chilean Patagonia — before they go extinct

WaPageTitle.value: Discovering new species in Chilean Patagonia

metadescription.value: Biologist Vreni Häussermann has been exploring and protecting the marine life of Chilean Patagonia for over 20 years. Read more on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Discovering new species in Chilean Patagonia

description.value: Executive director of CyberTracker Conservation, South African scientist and Rolex Awards Laureate Louis Liebenberg developed a piece of free software that allows traditional animal trackers in the Kalahari to share tracking insights and create better data on biodiversity. In return, trackers are paid for their work, turning a way of life into a vocation. Since its initial roll out in 1997, the app has evolved and migrated far from Southern Africa. It has been downloaded more than half a million times in over 200 countries, says its creator.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - The app securing Africa's wildlife

pageCaption.value: Indigenous trackers are teaching scientists about wildlife

WaPageTitle.value: Indigenous trackers

metadescription.value: Rolex Laureate Louis Liebenberg developed an app to share animal tracking insights and create better data on biodiversity. Learn more on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Indigenous trackers

description.value: Technologist and Rolex Associate Laureate Topher White turns old phones into recording devices that listen to the rainforest, helping to prevent illegal logging. Through his non-profit Rainforest Connection, White repackages old Android smartphones into a recycled plastic box fitted with an extra microphone, battery pack and solar panels. The finished devices look like mechanical flowers and are fastened to trees high up in the canopy, often up to 150 feet. They record sounds 24 hours a day from up to a mile away.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - New ways of protecting the rainforest

pageCaption.value: How old cell phones are spying on illegal loggers

WaPageTitle.value: New plastic recycling method

metadescription.value: Technologist Topher White has found a way to protect rainforests from illegal logging and poaching. Discover how on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Spying on illegal loggers

description.value: Tomas Diagne is saving the African turtles. The biologist and Rolex Associate Laureate opened Senegal's first rescue facility to revive endangered turtles and has produced award-winning results.

The world is in the midst of a plastic pollution crisis, but tech entrepreneur and Rolex Awards Laureate Miranda Wang has developed a process to turn non-recyclable plastics into virgin-grade chemicals that can be used to make new products. The mission of her company, BioCellection, to “make plastic waste infinitely recyclable,” says Wang. “We live in a plastic age, and we can't avoid that material ... (But) frankly our world hasn't been moving forward in innovating plastic recycling for the past decades.”

pageTitle.value: Rolex Partnerships - New plastic recycling method

pageCaption.value: Start-up devours pollution with new plastic recycling method

WaPageTitle.value: New plastic recycling method

metadescription.value: Recycling plastic has been a stop-start endeavor. Rolex Laureate Miranda Wang and her company, BioCellection, want to change that. More on rolex.org.
